reference: GATE CS — Computer Networks / Application Layer

map.condition: Which protocol uses TCP vs UDP
map.use: \text{HTTP, FTP, SMTP, Telnet, SSH} \to \text{TCP};\quad \text{DNS, DHCP, SNMP, TFTP} \to \text{UDP}

map.condition: DNS reverse lookup of IP a.b.c.d

map.use: \text{Send UDP/ICMP with TTL}=1,2,3,\ldots; \text{collect TTL-exceeded ICMP from each router}

map.condition: SMTP vs POP3 vs IMAP role
map.use: \text{SMTP: send/relay mail}\quad \text{POP3/IMAP: retrieve mail from server}

formulas.name: DNS: Hostname to IP Resolution
formulas.plain: DNS resolves a human-readable hostname (e.g. www.example.com) to an IP address via iterative or recursive queries
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what DNS does, or how a browser finds the IP for a URL

terms.means: client-side DNS component that initiates queries
server answers on behalf of client by querying other servers
server returns best referral; client contacts next server itself

formulas.trap: DNS primarily uses UDP port 53; TCP is used only for large responses (>512 bytes) or zone transfers. GATE often tests this distinction.

formulas.name: DNS Reverse Lookup
formulas.plain: To reverse-lookup IP a.b.c.d: reverse the octets and append .in-addr.arpa, then query PTR record
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks how to find the hostname for a given IP address (traceroute does this internally)

terms.means: special DNS zone for reverse IP lookups
DNS record type mapping IP back to hostname

formulas.trap: Octets are reversed in the query name: 168.128.16.5 becomes 5.16.128.168.in-addr.arpa — a classic GATE question.

formulas.name: DNS Hierarchy
formulas.plain: Root servers -> TLD servers (e.g. .com) -> Authoritative servers -> IP answer
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing the path of a DNS query from a client with no cached information

terms.means: Top-Level Domain server (e.g. .com, .org, .edu)
server that holds the definitive record for a domain

formulas.trap: Root servers do NOT know the final IP; they only refer to TLD servers. The actual IP comes from the authoritative server.

formulas.name: DNS Record Types
formulas.latex: \text{A: hostname}\to\text{IPv4} \quad \text{AAAA: hostname}\to\text{IPv6} \quad \text{MX: mail server} \quad \text{CNAME: alias} \quad \text{PTR: reverse}
formulas.plain: A=IPv4 address | AAAA=IPv6 address | MX=mail exchanger | CNAME=canonical name alias | PTR=reverse lookup | NS=name server
formulas.whenToUse: When asked what type of DNS record a given operation uses

terms.means: Mail eXchanger — DNS record pointing to the mail server for a domain
Canonical NAME — alias pointing to another hostname

formulas.trap: MX records point to hostnames, not IPs directly; a separate A/AAAA lookup is needed for the mail server IP.

formulas.name: HTTP Request Methods
formulas.latex: \text{GET: retrieve} \quad \text{POST: submit data} \quad \text{HEAD: headers only} \quad \text{PUT: upload} \quad \text{DELETE: remove}
formulas.plain: GET: retrieve resource | POST: submit data (body) | HEAD: headers only (no body) | PUT: upload/replace | DELETE: remove resource
formulas.whenToUse: When a GATE question asks which HTTP method is used for a specific action, or matching command to protocol

terms.means: same as GET but server returns only headers, no body — used to check resource metadata

formulas.trap: HEAD returns only headers (no response body). PROMPT is an FTP command, not HTTP. GATE 2014 question tests HEAD vs GET.

formulas.name: HTTP Non-Persistent vs Persistent Connections
formulas.latex: \text{Non-persistent: 1 TCP connection per object} \quad \text{Persistent: multiple objects per connection}
formulas.plain: Non-persistent HTTP/1.0: new TCP connection (1 RTT + 1 RTT data) per object. Persistent HTTP/1.1: reuse connection for multiple objects.
formulas.whenToUse: When computing time to load a web page with multiple objects given RTT

terms.means: Round-Trip Time for one message to travel to server and back

formulas.trap: Non-persistent HTTP requires 2 RTTs per object (1 for TCP setup + 1 for request/response) plus transmission time. With pipelining, persistent HTTP allows multiple concurrent requests.

formulas.name: FTP Control and Data Ports
formulas.latex: \text{Control connection: TCP port }21 \quad \text{Data connection: TCP port }20\text{ (active mode)}
formulas.plain: FTP uses two TCP connections: port 21 for control (commands/responses) and port 20 for data transfer (active mode)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about FTP port numbers or the two-channel architecture of FTP

terms.means: server initiates data connection from port 20 to client's ephemeral port
client initiates both connections; server listens on a random port for data

formulas.trap: FTP uses TWO separate TCP connections (unlike HTTP which uses one). RCPT is an SMTP command, not FTP. PROMPT is an FTP interactive command.

formulas.name: FTP Commands (GATE-tested)
formulas.latex: \text{RETR: download file} \quad \text{STOR: upload file} \quad \text{PROMPT: toggle interactive mode} \quad \text{LIST: directory listing}
formulas.plain: RETR=download | STOR=upload | PROMPT=toggle interactive prompting | LIST=list directory | USER=authenticate username
formulas.whenToUse: GATE matching questions that associate commands with protocols — PROMPT is FTP-specific

terms.means: FTP command that toggles whether FTP prompts user for confirmation on multi-file operations

formulas.trap: PROMPT is an FTP command, HEAD is HTTP, RCPT is SMTP — GATE 2012 tested exactly this three-way association.

formulas.name: SMTP Commands
formulas.plain: SMTP dialog: HELO -> MAIL FROM:<sender> -> RCPT TO:<recipient> -> DATA (message body) -> QUIT
formulas.whenToUse: When a GATE question asks which command belongs to SMTP, or the sequence of SMTP commands

terms.means: SMTP command specifying the recipient(s) of the email
SMTP command specifying the sender's address

formulas.trap: RCPT is an SMTP command (not FTP or HTTP). SMTP uses TCP port 25. SMTP is for sending/relaying mail — POP3/IMAP are for receiving.

formulas.name: Email Protocol Roles
formulas.latex: \text{SMTP (port 25): send/relay} \quad \text{POP3 (port 110): download \& delete} \quad \text{IMAP (port 143): sync \& manage on server}
formulas.plain: SMTP=send/relay (TCP 25); POP3=download and optionally delete from server (TCP 110); IMAP=manage mail on server, sync across devices (TCP 143)
formulas.whenToUse: Matching email protocols to their functions (send vs retrieve) or port numbers

terms.means: Post Office Protocol v3 — downloads mail to local client
Internet Message Access Protocol — keeps mail on server, synced across clients

formulas.trap: SMTP cannot be used to retrieve email; it only sends/relays. POP3 typically deletes mail from server after download; IMAP keeps it on the server.

formulas.name: Traceroute Mechanism
formulas.latex: \text{Send probes with TTL}=1,2,3,\ldots; \text{ each router returns ICMP Time-Exceeded when TTL}=0
formulas.plain: Traceroute sends packets with increasing TTL (1, 2, 3, ...); each router decrements TTL and sends ICMP Time-Exceeded when TTL hits 0, revealing the router's IP
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how traceroute discovers intermediate routers/hops between source and destination

terms.means: Time To Live — hop count field in IP header, decremented by each router
error message sent back when TTL reaches 0

formulas.trap: Traceroute discovers routers by exploiting TTL expiry and ICMP, NOT by querying DNS or routing tables directly. The source uses the ICMP source IP to identify each hop.

formulas.name: PING (ICMP Echo)
formulas.latex: \text{PING: sends ICMP Echo Request} \to \text{receives ICMP Echo Reply} \Rightarrow \text{RTT and reachability}
formulas.plain: PING uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply to test reachability and measure round-trip time to a host
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which protocol or message type ping uses, or how to test basic connectivity

terms.means: Internet Control Message Protocol — carries error and diagnostic messages at network layer

formulas.trap: PING uses ICMP (not TCP or UDP). ICMP operates at the Network layer (Layer 3), not Transport. A firewall blocking ICMP will make a host appear unreachable to ping even if it is up.

formulas.name: DHCP DORA Process
formulas.plain: DHCP: client broadcasts Discover -> server sends Offer -> client sends Request -> server sends Acknowledge (DORA)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about the DHCP process for auto-assigning an IP address to a new host

terms.means: Discover-Offer-Request-Acknowledge — four steps of DHCP address assignment
DHCP uses UDP: server on port 67, client on port 68

formulas.trap: DHCP uses UDP (not TCP) because the client has no IP yet when it sends the Discover broadcast. All four DORA messages are broadcast or unicast at UDP layer.

formulas.name: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)
formulas.latex: \text{SNMP: manager polls agents via UDP 161 to monitor/manage network devices (MIB)}
formulas.plain: SNMP allows a network manager to monitor and control network devices (routers, switches) via UDP port 161; data is stored in MIB
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks which protocol is used for network management or which transport protocol SNMP uses

terms.means: Management Information Base — structured collection of device variables accessible via SNMP
software running on managed device that responds to SNMP queries

formulas.trap: SNMP uses UDP (not TCP) for polling. SNMP traps (unsolicited alerts from agent to manager) use UDP port 162.
